titleStmt.title: Letter from Helen Duffin to Celia Duffin, 16 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Helen

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Helen Duffin (b.1891) to Celia Duffin (1883-1979). Helen provides news from home about the coming and going of various people. She seems particularly impressed by the rank of a number of officers.This letter is part of a rich correspondence between various members of the Duffin family, a large prosperous family living in Strandtown, Belfast. Several family members served in the war, including Major Terence Duffin, who served as a staff officer with 107 Brigade, and later with Royal Irish Rifles; Major Charles G Duffin MC, Royal Field Artillery; and their sisters Emma, Celia and Dorothy who served as Voluntary Aid Detachments (VADS) in Egypt and France, and with the YMCA. Celia Duffin was a VAD with the 3rd London General Hospital in Wandsworth which opened in 1914. She was an accomplished poet, publishing, amongst other volumes, a book of poetry with her sister Ruth.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Dunowen, Belfast 16/3/16 My dear Celia, Thank you very much for
sending the gazettes they are quite amusing.
How do you like nursing the Editor? I am glad you saw Olive and had
a day off. Dorothy is by way of going
to England to-morrow. She meant
to go to the Swannicks first, but
Hilda has been near some case of
German measles & is afraid she may
take it so D. plans now to go to
Epsom if Aunt Fanny can have
her. I suppose you will see her soon.
I wish to goodness Olive could get
another job I think her present one
is a dreadful life. Dorothy & I went for a walk with
Marjorie on Tuesday â poor dear â it is
a pity to see her. She evidently can
make no plans & does not know what
to do with herself. Charley has got
a job at home or rather he is
doing a course in England before
taking command of his battery. I hope
Marjorie may join him until he
goes back again. Did you hear that Elsie Kingan
Dunowen, Belfast
turned up for tea here yesterday. Molly
& I are helping at a buffet for
Mrs Campbell. We have to go to the
Midland Station once a month &
give buns, coffee, etc. to soldiers.
Peters' & Molly's photographs are
to come to-night â I'll send you
one if they are good. Don't forget your shamrocks
to-morrow! Ever yours Helen
